images.copyright: View from Skierffe Mountain over the Rapadalen river delta, Sarek National Park, Laponia, Lapland, Sweden (© Robert Haasmann/Getty Images)
images.title: Info
images.caption: Sarek National Park, Laponia, Lapland, Sweden
images.desc: If nature had a 'do not disturb' sign, it would hang over Sarek National Park, often called 'the last European wilderness.' This remote corner of Swedish Lapland reminds us what the planet looks like when humans take a step back—and let the wild take the lead.

images.copyright: Snowy egret preening, central Florida, United States (© Donald M. Jones/Minden Pictures)
images.title: Info
images.caption: Snowy egret preening, central Florida, United States
images.desc: Poetic name, poetic appearance. Meet the snowy egret, whose all-white plumage and bright yellow 'slippers' on black legs give it a striking look. Once nearly wiped out by demand for its delicate breeding-season feathers—soft, lacy plumes prized for fashionable hats—conservation efforts saved its plumage.

images.copyright: Cyclist in Bardenas Reales Natural Park and Biosphere Reserve, Navarra, Spain (© Artur Debat/Getty Images)
images.title: Info
images.caption: Bardenas Reales Natural Park and Biosphere Reserve, Navarra, Spain
images.desc: An adventurer shown here cycles through Bardenas Reales Natural Park and Biosphere Reserve in Navarra, Spain, a stark landscape shaped by wind and water over millions of years. Its maze of eroded cliffs, plateaus and dry channels feels closer to another planet than northern Spain. Ever wondered why filmmakers use it as a stand-in for deserts and distant worlds? The reserve covers more than 400 square kilometres and supports hardy plants adapted to heat, drought and poor soils. Griffon vultures circle above ravines, while foxes, wildcats and migratory birds move across the semi-arid terrain.

og.title: Bardenas Reales Natural Park and Biosphere Reserve, Navarra, Spain

images.copyright: Highway through Xitai Jinaier Lake, Qinghai Province, China (© Kaicheng Xu/Getty Images)
images.title: Info
images.caption: Highway through Xitai Jinaier Lake, Qinghai Province, China
images.desc: Cutting a surreal path through the high-altitude deserts of western China, Xitai Jinaier Lake (also known as West Taijinar Lake) is a striking salt lake in the remote Qaidam Basin. A stretch of National Highway 315 runs directly across its shallow waters, creating the illusion of a road floating between two worlds. On one side, the water often appears deep blue; on the other, a softer green hue, a contrast shaped by differing mineral concentrations in the brine.

og.title: Highway through Xitai Jinaier Lake, Qinghai Province, China

images.copyright: Palermo skyline at dusk, Sicily, Italy (© Sean Pavone/Getty Images)
images.title: Info
images.caption: Palermo, Sicily, Italy
images.desc: Palermo, the capital of the island of Sicily in southern Italy, is a city where layers of history, art and culture meet in a vivid and sometimes chaotic harmony. Founded by the Phoenicians and later shaped by Romans, Arabs, Normans and Spaniards, it carries traces of many civilisations that passed through the Mediterranean. This rich past is visible in its architecture, from the Arab-Norman palaces and churches recognised by UNESCO to the Baroque facades that define its historic streets.

images.copyright: Aerial view of Everglades National Park, Florida, United States (© Tetra Images/Getty Images)
images.title: Info
images.caption: Everglades National Park, Florida, United States
images.desc: Few places show their power quite like Everglades National Park in Florida, United States. From above, Florida's iconic wetlands unfold as a vast patchwork of shallow water, sawgrass, mangroves and winding channels, shaped by freshwater flowing slowly south from Lake Okeechobee.

images.copyright: Plains zebra foal in Etosha National Park, Namibia (© Sharon Heald/Nature Picture Library)
images.title: Info
images.caption: Plains zebra foal in Etosha National Park, Namibia
images.desc: Etosha National Park, Namibia, is not a place for soft starts, and the plains zebra foal in this image seems to get the memo. In a land this vast—nearly 22,300 square kilometres of salt pans and grassland—timing is everything, and hesitation is expensive. Lions, spotted hyenas, wild dogs, cheetahs and leopards constantly pressure the herd—especially the youngest—so zebras are born ready. Minutes after arrival, they're upright. Hours later, they're moving.

images.copyright: Mount Everest summit, Sagarmatha National Park, Nepal (© fotoVoyager/Getty Images)
images.title: Info
images.caption: Mount Everest summit, Sagarmatha National Park, Nepal
images.desc: In Sagarmatha National Park, Nepal, Mount Everest rises into view, calm and unmoved as evening settles in. That stillness was broken in 1953. After years of planning and weeks of climbing, New Zealander Edmund Hillary and Sherpa mountaineer Tenzing Norgay reached the summit. At just under 8,850 metres above sea level, they became the first people known to have stood at Earth's highest point.
